paragraphs.en: Illustration by Henning Wagenbreth
paragraphs.zh: 插图：Henning Wagenbreth

paragraphs.en: Late as I was, I figured I’d be walking right into an active scenario. Crazy Omar, maybe. Or Outnumbered and Outgunned. I thought I’d hear the thud of stun grenades and hillbilly shouts of “Allahu akbar! ” I expected to taste the bitter clouds of cordite drifting over the lawn and see the green muzzle flash of machine guns firing blanks on full auto. Above all, I assumed I’d find wounded pigs dying on the grass, and the boys elbow deep in their salvation.
paragraphs.zh: 既然我已经迟到，我便想象自己将直接走进一个正在上演的场景。也许是疯狂的奥马尔，或是人少枪多的局面。我以为会听见震耳欲聋的震慑弹爆鸣，以及希尔伯利式的喊声：“Allahu akbar！”我还以为会闻到漂过草坪的苦涩火药烟云，并看到机枪全自动连发时在草地上划出的绿色火光。最重要的是，我原以为会看到受伤的猪倒在草地上，男孩们把手肘都埋进了所谓的救赎之中。

paragraphs.en: Turned out, however, that I hadn’t missed much. The pig men were in the trailer, knocking out the pigs with anesthesia. The pigs, perhaps sensing the looming betrayal, or at least recognizing a serious departure from their bottle-fed, sunny upbringing, fought back. Hooves banged against the metal floor. Squeals cut through the cool air. Steam leaked through the trailer’s slats. Pig fear had the same sour reek as our fear.
paragraphs.zh: 然而结果是，我并没有错过太多。那些养猪的人在拖车里，正在用麻醉剂把猪弄昏。那些猪也许察觉到了即将到来的背叛，或者至少意识到自己要离开那种被奶瓶喂养、阳光明媚的成长环境，它们开始反抗。蹄子重重撞击着金属地板。尖叫声切裂了清冷的空气。蒸汽从拖车的栏板缝里冒出。猪的恐惧有着和我们一样刺鼻的酸臭味。

paragraphs.en: I found the boys stooging in a patch of morning sun at the base of our compound’s tower.
paragraphs.zh: 我在我们基地塔楼底部一块晨光照在的草地里看到了那些男孩。

paragraphs.en: Ted Waters, with his real leg crossed over his mechanical one, eyeballed my approach. “Nice of you to join us,” he said.
paragraphs.zh: 泰德·沃特斯把他的真腿搭在机械义腿上，盯着我走来的方向看了看。“你能来真是帮了大忙，”他说。

paragraphs.en: Bing Thomas spoke out of the corner of his new mouth. “Look who deshided to show up.”
paragraphs.zh: 宾·托马斯从他那张新换的嘴一侧开口说：“看看是谁终于决定露面了。”

paragraphs.en: Read an interview with the author for the story behind the story.
paragraphs.zh: 请阅读作者的访谈，了解这个故事背后的故事。

paragraphs.en: I’d planned to arrive early, before the boys and before the pig men, just as I had for the previous pig labs I’d attended in preparation for my troop’s deployments. I liked to give myself time to prepare for the day’s events. The scenarios were nothing more than window dressing—meant to evoke the chaotic night raids that would be our mission downrange. It was the pigs that had me worried. A pig’s face might remind me of Bing’s old face. Or a pig’s leg lying off to the side, detached and jittering, could resemble Ted’s leg. I’d wanted to arrive early enough to think through these possibilities and allow other, half-forgotten things to bubble to the surface. But, just as I was about to leave the house, my wife, Allison, had screamed from the upstairs bedroom.
paragraphs.zh: 我本想提前到达，在那些男孩和所谓“猪人”之前，像以前参加过的几次“猪实验室”一样提前做准备，因为这是我小队部署前的惯例。我喜欢给自己留出时间，提前适应当天的安排。这些场景不过是“门面”——用来营造我们在前线执行任务时会遭遇的混乱夜袭氛围。真正让我紧张的是这些“猪”。一头猪的脸让我想起了宾昔日的面容；而一只侧卧、脱离身体、颤抖着的猪腿，又会让我想起泰德的腿。我原本想早点到，把这些联想提前想清楚，让那些半被遗忘的念头浮上来。可就在我正要离开家时，妻子艾莉森从楼上的卧室里突然尖叫起来。

paragraphs.en: “Circumstances beyond my control,” I explained to the boys.
paragraphs.zh: 我向那些男孩解释说：“这是我控制不了的情况。”

paragraphs.en: The trailer’s back gate swung open, and a pig man wearing a blacksmith’s apron emerged. He’d been blown up, I guessed, then put back together minus some pieces. I suspected this because a curious light shone from around his joints, as if some kind of spell held him together, specifically the type of spell that would break if anyone drew attention to it. He wore a red beard that looked like it had been carved out of wood, dipped in wax, and clicked into place. He approached us on a curve.
paragraphs.zh: 拖车的后门一掀开，一位戴着铁匠围裙的猪人走了出来。我猜他似乎被炸过，然后被重新拼回去，只是少了几块。我之所以这么想，是因为他关节周围冒出一种奇异的光，像是有什么法术把他维系在一起，而且这种法术一旦有人把注意力集中到它上面就会失效。他留着一撮红胡子，看起来像是刻在木头上、蘸过蜡后再“咔嗒”一声就扣在脸上的样子。他走向我们时像是沿着弧线慢慢逼近。

paragraphs.en: “Who’s done this before?” he asked.
paragraphs.zh: “这是谁干的？”他说。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard spoke in a low, electric monotone. “Someone besides him, then. Why are we here?”
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子用低沉、带电般的平板声说：“那就不是他做的。那我们为什么会在这里？”

paragraphs.en: Officially, we were there to learn—via live, unconscious, anesthetized, and wounded pigs—how to manage battlefield amputations, hemorrhaging, sucking chest wounds, skull fractures, and the like. Unofficially, however, this was a sacrifice—pigs instead of us.
paragraphs.zh: 按官方说法，我们来这里是为了通过活猪、麻醉猪和受伤的猪学习如何处理战场截肢、失血、吸气性胸伤、颅骨骨折等问题；但非官方地说，这其实是一次“替罪”——牺牲的是猪，而不是我们自己。

paragraphs.en: An answer to Red Beard’s question came from behind me: “We’re here to fuck up some pigs.”
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子的问题得到了一个从我身后传来的回答：‘我们是来把这些猪整得一团糟的。’

paragraphs.en: “Who said that?” Red Beard asked.
paragraphs.zh: “是谁这么说的？”红胡子问道。

paragraphs.en: It was the new guy. Untested as he was, his body was whole, and his mind was intact. As a result, his voice was confident, clear, and amplified by an empty-headed belief in his own good intentions.
paragraphs.zh: 那是个新来的人。虽然他未经考验，但他的身体完好，心智也毫发无损。于是他的语气便显得自信、清晰，并且被一种空洞却坚定的善意信念所放大。

paragraphs.en: New Guy tried to fix himself. “It was a stupid joke,” he said.
paragraphs.zh: 新来的人试图弥补自己的过失。“那只是个愚蠢的玩笑，”他说。

paragraphs.en: From the pig trailer came a bang, followed by a muffled squeal.
paragraphs.zh: 从猪篷车里传来一声巨响，接着是被压抑的尖叫。

paragraphs.en: “These pigs are our brothers, not jokes,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: “这些猪都是我们的兄弟，不是笑料。”红胡子说。

paragraphs.en: “I’m sorry,” New Guy said. “I wasn’t thinking.”
paragraphs.zh: “对不起，”新来的人说。“我当时没想清楚。”

paragraphs.en: Red Beard nodded, and a flash of light escaped from a scar on his neck.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子点了点头，脖子上的一道伤疤里闪出一道亮光。

paragraphs.en: “Sorry’s not good enough. You can leave.”
paragraphs.zh: “道歉还不够。你可以走了。”

paragraphs.en: Podcast: The Writer’s VoiceListen to Will Mackin read “Pig Lab.”
paragraphs.zh: 播客 The Writer’s Voice：收听 Will Mackin 诵读《Pig Lab》

paragraphs.en: Allison’s scream was not that of a full-grown woman, the kind I’d heard on so many night raids. There was no depth to it, no hint of recognition of the horror that had provoked it. It was, instead, the simple, guileless scream of a young girl, which meant that my wife was having a flashback.
paragraphs.zh: 艾莉森的尖叫不像成年女人那样有分量，这种尖叫声我在许多夜间突袭里都听过。它没有丝毫深沉，也没有意识到引发这种尖叫的恐怖的痕迹。反而，它是一个小女孩那种天真无瑕的尖叫，这意味着我的妻子正在经历一次闪回。

paragraphs.en: I ran upstairs and into the bedroom, where I found the bed empty, the covers flung aside.
paragraphs.zh: 我冲上楼去到卧室，发现床上是空的，被褥被扔到一边。

paragraphs.en: I got on my hands and knees to look under the bed, which was supposed to be Allison’s safe place. This had been decided in therapy, which we’d signed up for after Allison had got pregnant, and her memories, which she’d suppressed for decades, had started to become a problem. The therapist was a round, soft woman who sat cross-legged on a fuzzy couch. Allison and I sat on a smooth couch opposite her. There was a glass coffee table between us, and under that was a cardboard box full of toys. A big clock hung on the wall, ticking softly.
paragraphs.zh: 我趴在床边，俯身去看床底，因为那本来是艾莉森的“安全角”。这是我们在艾莉森怀孕后开始做治疗时决定的，因为她那些被压抑了几十年的记忆开始带来麻烦。治疗师是个圆润、和善的女人，盘腿坐在一张毛绒绒的沙发上。艾莉森和我坐在她对面的光滑沙发上。我们中间放着一张玻璃茶几，茶几下面放着一个装满玩具的纸板箱。墙上挂着一只大钟，发出轻轻的滴答声。

paragraphs.en: The therapist said that Allison needed a place to go where she would feel protected. When Allison couldn’t think of a safe place on her own, the therapist suggested under the bed. I wanted to hug my wife and tell her that everything was going to be all right. I wanted to convince her of this with the kind of hug that was a safe space in and of itself, that would feel like the inverse of all those dark and musty spaces that the boys and I, while rooting out the enemy downrange, had torn big loud holes in. But, as the therapist explained to me, Allison needed to be in a place where no one would try to convince her of anything.
paragraphs.zh: 治疗师说艾莉森需要一个地方去，让她觉得自己很安全。当艾莉森自己想不出这样一个安全的地方时，治疗师建议她躲到床底下。我想抱紧妻子，告诉她一切都会好起来。我想用一种本身就像避风港的拥抱来让她真正相信——那种拥抱应该能和我们和那些男孩们在外头清除敌人的时候，在又黑又潮湿的地方挖出的巨大、闷响的洞形成对比。但正如治疗师向我解释的，艾莉森需要待在一个没人会试图向她灌输任何想法的地方。

paragraphs.en: I didn’t find her under the bed. I didn’t find her in the bathroom, either. She wasn’t curled up under the toilet tank, where I’d discovered her once before. Nor was she lying fetal and naked in the cold and empty tub.
paragraphs.zh: 我没有在床底下找到她。浴室里也没有。我没有在马桶水箱下找到她，尽管我曾经有一次在那儿发现过她。也没看到她蜷缩在冷冰冰、空荡荡的浴缸里，像胎儿一样赤裸着躺着。

paragraphs.en: In the four months that Allison had been pregnant, I’d learned a few of the things that triggered her flashbacks: Her father’s birthday was one. Her mother’s birthday, just two days later, was another. The smell of spoiled milk (which, until age thirteen, she had thought was the smell of fresh milk); and the sight of the little girl from down the street, standing off to the side, away from the other kids at the bus stop, with her dirty face, uncombed hair, dingy stockings, and untied shoes.
paragraphs.zh: 在艾莉森怀孕的四个月里，我学会了几件会触发她“闪回”的事：她父亲的生日就是其中之一；她母亲的生日紧接着两天后也是；发馊的牛奶味道（她一直以为直到十三岁之前那股味道就是新鲜牛奶的味道）；以及隔壁街上那个小女孩的样子——她在校车站会站在一边、远离其他孩子，脸上脏兮兮的、头发蓬松未梳、袜子发黄发脏、鞋子没系紧。

paragraphs.en: Allison was thirty-one years old, tall with long auburn hair and a bright smile. In the throes of a flashback, however, she became a terrified child. Her heart hammered away, her eyes flickered, and her voice grew tiny and plaintive. The flashbacks often happened at dawn, as the world was coming into focus, or in the evening, when it was starting to dim. The moment a flashback began, Allison would hide. I did not turn on the lights to find her, and I did not call her name, because these were things that her father had done. Instead, I stood quietly and listened.
paragraphs.zh: 艾莉森三十一岁，身材高挑，长着一头赤褐色长发，笑容明亮。然而一旦陷入闪回，她就会变成一个惊恐的小孩。她的心怦怦直跳，眼神一闪一闪，声音也会变得很轻、很怯生。闪回常常发生在黎明，世界刚刚清晰起来的时候，或是傍晚，天色开始转暗的时候。每当闪回刚开始，艾莉森就会躲起来。我不敢开灯去找她，也不敢喊她的名字，因为这些都是她父亲当年做过的事。相反，我只是静静站着，倾听。

paragraphs.en: Explosions had all but ruined my hearing. As a result, there was no such thing as quiet. Sometimes crickets chirped in my head. Other times, a phantom string quartet warmed up. Standing in our bedroom that morning, I heard a pot of water rumbling to a boil.
paragraphs.zh: 爆炸几乎彻底毁掉了我的听力。结果就是，几乎没有“安静”这回事。有时我脑中会响起蟋蟀鸣叫；有时又会听见一支“幻听”的弦乐四重奏在热身。那天早上我站在卧室里时，听见一锅水在咕噜咕噜地烧开。

paragraphs.en: Then, from the baby’s room, I heard a whimper and a thump.
paragraphs.zh: 随后，从婴儿房里传来一声呜咽和一声闷响。

paragraphs.en: Pigs were lined up on their backs on the grass, anesthetized, mouths wide open, tongues hanging out, eyes slammed shut. Armed pig men stood at either end of the line. Closest to the fence was a pig man with a rubber hand, who was gripping a hatchet with his flesh-and-blood hand. The pig man at the other end of the line wore an eye patch and carried an M4. These two stood ready to wound the pigs as soon as Red Beard was done saying goodbye.
paragraphs.zh: 猪只一列仰卧在草地上，已经被麻醉，嘴大张着，舌头垂在外面，眼睛紧闭着。两端各站着持枪的“猪人”。离栅栏最近的是戴着橡皮手的猪人，他用自己有血有肉的那只手紧握着一把柴刀。队列另一端的那位戴着眼罩、背着一支M4步枪。等红胡子说完告别，这两个人就会立刻动手伤害这些猪。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard knelt alongside a pig, kissed the top of its head, and gave it a hug. Slowly, he rose to his feet and moved on to the next pig.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子蹲在一头猪旁边，轻轻亲了它的头顶，又抱了它一下。随后，他慢慢站起身，走向下一头猪。

paragraphs.en: I walked over to where Red Beard was kneeling. Eye Patch rocked on the balls of his feet and hummed. Sunlight chimed off Rubber Hand’s hatchet blade. Red Beard’s palm rested on the pig’s chest, right above its heart. My shadow fell on him.
paragraphs.zh: 我走到红胡子跪着的地方。眼罩那个家伙踮着脚，轻轻摇晃着，还哼着歌。阳光在橡皮手那把柴刀的刀刃上闪出金属般清脆的反光。红胡子的手掌放在那头猪的胸口，正好在心脏上方。我的影子落在了他身上。

paragraphs.en: “What do you say we call this off?” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “我们要不要就此作罢？”我说。

paragraphs.en: The sun, directly behind me, gave my shadow a halo. Red Beard looked up at me. “How so?” he asked.
paragraphs.zh: 太阳正好在我身后，把我的影子映成了一道光晕。红胡子抬头看着我。“怎么回事？”他问道。

paragraphs.en: “You take the pigs back to the farm, and I take the boys back to the compound. If higher asks me how the training went, I’ll say it was great.”
paragraphs.zh: “把那些猪送回农场，我再把这些男孩带回营地。如果上面有人问我训练进展如何，我会说进展得很顺利。”

paragraphs.en: “But your men haven’t done the training.”
paragraphs.zh: “可你的人都还没做训练。”

paragraphs.en: “Yeah, they have. All of them did it, except the guy you sent home.”
paragraphs.zh: “做了。”

paragraphs.en: “None of them raised their hands.”
paragraphs.zh: “他们一个人也没有举手。”

paragraphs.en: Cartoon by Liam Francis Walsh
paragraphs.zh: Liam Francis Walsh 的漫画

paragraphs.en: “They don’t like raising their hands. It makes them feel stupid.”
paragraphs.zh: “他们不喜欢举手。那会让他们觉得自己很蠢。”

paragraphs.en: “What about good order and discipline?”
paragraphs.zh: “那纪律和秩序怎么办？”

paragraphs.en: The light that shone from Red Beard’s joints brightened.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子身上的伤口光芒更亮了。

paragraphs.en: “Were you like this before?” I asked.
paragraphs.zh: “你以前也是这样吗？”我问道。

paragraphs.en: I had been walking behind Ted Waters on that blue night, along the highway outside Marjah, in Afghanistan, when the bomb took his leg. The explosion had balled me up, shoved me aside, and pelted me with hot stones. Aside from a sharp ringing in my ears, however, I was fine. I’d knelt next to Ted as he lay on his back, conscious and bleeding on the pavement. “What happened?” he’d asked me as I fastened a tourniquet around his tattered thigh, then looked at my watch. It was twenty-three minutes after midnight, so I wrote “0023” in grease pencil on Ted’s forehead. “You’re going to be fine,” I’d said. But Ted had already begun to realize that he was not going to be fine. And every now and then since, whenever things were quiet and no one was paying attention, he thought back to a time when he was fine. Now it seemed as if Red Beard were remembering, too.
paragraphs.zh: 那是在阿富汗马尔贾外的公路上、那次“蓝色的夜晚”里，我跟在泰德·沃特斯（Ted Waters）身后走时发生的，后来炸弹夺去了他的腿。爆炸把我卷成一团，狠狠推到一边，还把我击打得满身是热石。除了耳中一阵尖锐的嗡鸣，我其实还好。我跪在泰德身旁，他平躺在柏油路上，清醒着却在流血。我给他那条破烂的腿上系上止血带，同时看了看手表，已经过了午夜二十三分钟，于是在泰德的额头上用油性铅笔写下“0023”。“你会没事的。”我当时说。可是泰德早已开始意识到自己不会没事。此后每当环境安静、没人注意时，他时不时就会回想自己曾经健康无恙的那段时光。现在看起来，红胡子似乎也在回想了。

paragraphs.en: “You can’t choose the things you want to do versus those things you don’t,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子说：“你不能只挑你想做的事和你不想做的事。”

paragraphs.en: “My wife is pregnant,” I told him.
paragraphs.zh: “我和他说：‘我老婆怀孕了。’

paragraphs.en: “Congratulations,” Red Beard said. “I have a newborn son myself.”
paragraphs.zh: “恭喜。”红胡子说。“我自己也有一个新生儿儿子。”

paragraphs.en: After Ted Waters and Bing Thomas were wounded, I put them both on injured reserve, thinking that I was doing them a favor. They could retire early with full benefits, and they could have a go at becoming normal citizens. But they refused. They both wanted back in. I thought this was insane, so I made them an insane proposal. If they could get through Hell Week again, I’d sign waivers for the face and the leg. It wasn’t pretty, but they made it.
paragraphs.zh: 泰德·沃特斯和宾·托马斯受伤后，我把他们都放进了伤残保留名单，我当时以为自己是在帮他们。我本以为这样对他们最好，他们可以提前退役并拿到完整福利，尝试过上普通人的生活。可他们都拒绝了，他们都想回来。我觉得这太疯狂了，于是向他们提出了一个疯狂的条件：如果他们能再熬过“地狱周”，我就替他们签署面部和腿部的豁免同意书。这样做并不光彩，但他们做到了。

paragraphs.en: “We could save them both,” I said to Red Beard. “My kid and your kid. We could stop this nonsense right now and maybe they won’t have to go to war.”
paragraphs.zh: “我们可以把他们俩都救下来，”我对红胡子说，“我的孩子和你的孩子。我们现在就能让这场荒唐事停下来，也许他们就不用上战场了。”

paragraphs.en: “You take the pigs back to the farm, and I take the boys back to the compound. If higher asks me how the training went, I’ll say it was great.”
paragraphs.zh: “你把这些猪带回农场，而我把那些男孩带回营区。如果上级问我训练进展如何，我会说非常成功。”

paragraphs.en: I thought of the big dry-erase board hanging in our ops shed, where all our names, specialties, and requirements were gridded out in black marker. A green check mark in a box meant that a particular block of refresher training, required prior to our next deployment—Marksmanship, Free Fall, Clandestine Entry—had been completed. An open box meant incomplete. Bing Thomas had drawn this grid by hand, following each warped line with a slightly more warped line until the grid practically curled under itself. Pig Lab was the last column on the grid. Its deformed and empty boxes appeared to have been crushed under a landslide of green checks.
paragraphs.zh: 我想起了我们作业棚里挂着的一块大白板，上面用黑色记号笔把我们所有人的姓名、专长和要求都划分成方格。某个方格里打了绿色勾号，就表示下一次部署前必须完成的某项复训——射击、自由跳伞、隐秘入场——已经完成；空着的方格则表示未完成。Bing Thomas 用手画了这张网格，一条一条地沿着每条歪曲的线画出更歪曲的线，直到网格几乎自己卷了起来。Pig Lab 在这张网格里的位置是最后一列。它那扭曲又空空的格子看起来像被一场绿色勾号的崩塌压在了下面。

paragraphs.en: “Then what?” Red Beard asked.
paragraphs.zh: “那接下来呢？”红胡子问。

paragraphs.en: Of course I’d imagined a world where pigs were humans and we were pigs. Where pigs fought wars while wanting to save their fellow-pigs from death. Where pigs learned from humans how to save themselves.
paragraphs.zh: 当然，我脑海里想象过这样一个世界：猪是人，而我们是猪。猪们参战，却只想把同胞从死亡中拯救出来。猪从人类那里学会了自救的方法。

paragraphs.en: In this upside-down world there’d be a pig like Ted Waters, who, one blue winter night on a concrete highway outside Marjah, had his leg blown off by a bomb disguised as a guardrail. And there’d be a pig like me, who’d knelt beside Ted and applied the tourniquet just as he’d been trained to do. Then, a month later, in Khost, a pig like Bing Thomas would have opened a booby-trapped door and had his face sheared off. And a pig like me would have picked Bing’s face up off the dirt, rinsed it in cold water from his canteen, and felt the skin flex in the palm of his hand.
paragraphs.zh: 在这个颠倒的世界里，会有一个像泰德·沃特斯那样的人——在马尔贾哈外一条混凝土公路上的一个寒冷的冬夜，他的腿被一颗伪装成护栏的炸弹炸掉了。也会有一个像我这样的“猪”，就像我曾经被训练过的那样，跪在泰德身边，给他做止血带。一个月后，在赫斯特，又会有一个像宾·托马斯那样的人，他会打开一扇被陷阱装置的门，结果脸被生生撕开。我这样一个“猪”就会把他的脸从泥土里捧起，用随身水壶里的冷水冲洗，然后感觉到皮肤在掌心里轻轻颤动。

paragraphs.en: Now I was on my back in the grass that grew dark and thick all around the tall, concrete tower. My jaw was slack, my eyes were slammed shut, and my tongue hung out, touching the ground. I tasted dirt and chlorophyll. The black sun shone through my tightly closed eyelids, and I could feel myself gently rising and falling as if I were lying in a rowboat on the ocean. A deep incision ran from the base of my throat to my navel. My sternum was fractured, my rib cage pried open. Cool shadows of puffy clouds floated across my exposed heart, which the pigs took turns touching with their snouts.
paragraphs.zh: 这时我仰面躺在草丛中，那些草围绕着高高的混凝土塔长得又黑又密。我的下巴松弛，眼睛紧闭，舌头伸出，碰着地面。我尝到了泥土和叶绿素的味道。黑色的太阳透过紧闭的眼睑洒下微光，我能感觉到自己轻轻起伏着，仿佛漂在海上的一条长排筏。我的伤口从喉咙根部一直划到肚脐，深深地切开。我的胸骨骨折，肋骨被掀开。蓬松的云影在我裸露的心脏上方轻轻飘过，几只“猪”轮流用鼻尖去碰它。

paragraphs.en: With their snouts, the pigs would feel each beat of my heart the way a human would feel a silver dollar that had been flipped in the air then caught in an open palm, flipped and caught, coming up heads or tails, whichever side had been called when the coin was at its apogee. The pigs would know, from my heart’s predictability, that the spark of life is durable. And they would learn that, no matter how bad a situation appears, it could always be worse. I didn’t understand their language, but I knew what the pigs were saying to one another, because it was the same stuff that the boys and the pig men say to one another.
paragraphs.zh: 这些“猪”用鼻子一下一下去触我的心跳，就像人会用手心接住一枚抛到空中的一角银元，反复抛起再接住，它是正面还是反面，取决于那枚硬币在空中最高点时喊的口令。它们从我的心脏稳定的节律中知道，生命的火花是经得起考验的。它们也会明白，无论处境看起来多么糟糕，总还有更糟的。虽然我不懂它们的语言，但我明白它们在彼此说些什么，因为那和男孩们与“猪男”之间说的话，是同一类内容。

paragraphs.en: New Pig, who had apparently been allowed back into the group after Red Pig had calmed down and accepted his apology for joking about their human brothers, touched my naked heart with his cold, wet snout, tickling me and making me snicker.
paragraphs.zh: 新来的“猪”看来在“红猪”冷静下来并接受他为取笑他们的人类兄弟而道过歉之后，已经被允许重新加入了队伍。他用自己冰冷、湿湿的鼻尖轻触我裸露的心脏，痒得我发出轻笑。

paragraphs.en: “This one is a rascal,” New Pig said, of me.
paragraphs.zh: “这是个坏小子，”新猪说起我来。

paragraphs.en: “You know it,” Red Pig said. “He opens doors with his hands.”
paragraphs.zh: “你知道的，”红猪说，“他会用手去开门。”

paragraphs.en: “Also plays fetch,” Eye-Patch Pig said.
paragraphs.zh: “还会捡回物。”眼罩猪说。

paragraphs.en: “I never heard of a human that could fetch,” New Pig said.
paragraphs.zh: “我从没听说过有人类会捡东西。”新猪说。

paragraphs.en: “You should come out to the farm sometime,” Red Pig said.
paragraphs.zh: “你改天总该来农场看看，”红猪说。

paragraphs.en: Mention of the farm brought to mind a make-believe oasis of sunshine, warm fields of thick, green grass, fat red apples hanging from a tree, oats mixed with brown sugar in the trough, and a cool, clear stream winding through the center of it all.
paragraphs.zh: 一提到农场，我脑中便浮现出一个虚构的绿洲：阳光灿烂、温暖的田野里长着茂密的绿色草地，树上挂着硕大的红苹果，木槽里混着燕麦和红糖，中央蜿蜒着一条清凉透明的小溪。

paragraphs.en: “Look,” Red Pig said, nodding toward me on the grass, my white heart thudding slowly in my open chest. “That’s a strong, steady heartbeat. He can keep going like that for a while.”
paragraphs.zh: “看，”红猪说着向我在草地上的位置点了点头，我那颗“白色”的心脏在敞开的胸口里慢慢有力地跳着。“那是很强而稳定的心跳。他可以这样持续跳一会儿。”

paragraphs.en: The sun disappeared behind a cloud, and I shivered. I thought of Allison lying in the cold and empty bathtub. As I lifted her out of the tub, she’d pleaded with me to stop touching her.
paragraphs.zh: 太阳躲进一朵云后面，我打了个冷颤。我想起艾莉森正躺在冰冷空荡荡的浴缸里。等我把她从浴缸里抱起来，她曾恳求我别再碰她。

paragraphs.en: “Everybody had a turn?” Red Pig asked. No one answered.
paragraphs.zh: “每个人都轮到了吗？”红猪问道。没有人回答。

paragraphs.en: “O.K.,” Red Pig said. “Anybody not had a turn, raise your hand.”
paragraphs.zh: “好，”红猪说。“如果有人还没轮到，请举手。”

paragraphs.en: The pigs just looked at him.
paragraphs.zh: 那些猪只是看着他。

paragraphs.en: I sat cross-legged on the shag carpet next to the blue toybox. Its wooden lid rattled.
paragraphs.zh: 我盘腿坐在蓝色玩具箱旁边的毛绒地毯上。它的木盖子发出哗啦声。

paragraphs.en: “No,” Allison said, from inside. She sounded small and far away.
paragraphs.zh: “不，”艾莉森在里面说。她的声音听起来又小又远。

paragraphs.en: “It’s O.K.,” I said. “It’s me.”
paragraphs.zh: “没关系，”我说。“是我。”

paragraphs.en: Allison had scooped all the toys out of the box and tossed them on the floor. New toys, like the spaceship and the motorcycle, were piled on old toys, like the cowboy I’d had as a kid.
paragraphs.zh: 艾莉森把盒子里的所有玩具都抓出来，随手扔到了地板上。新的玩具，比如那架宇宙飞船和那辆摩托车，压在旧玩具上面，比如我小时候那只牛仔玩偶。

paragraphs.en: Once upon a time, this cowboy had bent at the knees, elbows, shoulders, and hips. I’d taken him everywhere with me. Then one summer day, at the neighborhood pool, where I was taking swimming lessons, bullies had pulled my cowboy apart and left all his pieces in a glass ashtray. When I found him, I was inconsolable. I cried non-stop for the cowboy, but also for myself, and for my mother, my father, my brother, my sister. Death would not spare us, either.
paragraphs.zh: 从前，这个牛仔娃娃能在膝盖、肘部、肩膀和髋部弯曲。我带着他走过了很多地方。后来有一个夏天，在我去附近游泳池上游泳课的那天，有些恶霸把我的牛仔娃娃拆开，还把零件全都放在一个玻璃烟灰缸里。等我找到他时，我整个人都不可抑制地哭了起来。我为那只牛仔娃娃哭了个不停，也为我自己、我的妈妈、我的爸爸、我的弟弟、我的妹妹哭。死亡也不会放过我们。

paragraphs.en: My father used a powerful glue to reassemble the cowboy, which twenty-five years later still smelled like orange peels. The cowboy wore the brown vest that I’d cut for him out of felt. The shape of his face was unchanged, but its features, drawn in black ink on soft plastic, had faded away.
paragraphs.zh: 我父亲用一种强力胶把那个牛仔人偶重新拼接起来，这个人偶在二十五年后仍然带着橘子皮般的味道。牛仔穿着我给他用毡布剪出来的那件棕色背心。尽管他的脸型没变，但画在软质塑料上、用黑色墨水勾勒的五官已经褪色了。

paragraphs.en: “It’s Monday, September 8, 2009. We’re in our house on Rockrose Lane. You’re safe here,” I told Allison through the toybox lid.
paragraphs.zh: “现在是2009年9月8日，星期一。我们在洛克罗斯巷的家里。你在这里很安全，”我透过玩具盒的盖子对艾莉森说道。

paragraphs.en: “No-o-o-o,” she moaned.
paragraphs.zh: “不——呃——哦。”她呻吟道。

paragraphs.en: Allison wasn’t big by any stretch. Even pregnant, she maintained her lithe shape from her swimming days. And she’d always been flexible, but she still shouldn’t have fit in the toybox. In the throes of a flashback, however, she underwent some kind of transformation that allowed her to occupy otherwise impossible spaces. I’d seen something like this performed on a TV show called “That’s Incredible,” where a six-foot-tall, two-hundred-pound yogi had folded himself into a plexiglass cube half the size of the toybox. You could see the yogi through the transparent walls of the cube, his serene face surrounded by limbs like so many spare parts. Allison was similarly contorted whenever I found her mid-flashback. Unlike the yogi, however, she was unable to escape whatever predicament she’d got herself into. When she’d jammed herself under the toilet tank, for example, I’d had to drain the tank, remove it, and pull the bowl out of the floor to free her.
paragraphs.zh: 艾莉森一点也不高。即使怀孕了，她仍保留着从游泳时期带来的纤细线条。她一向很灵活，但也不该真能塞进玩具盒。可在一阵闪回发作时，她会发生某种变形，能钻进平时根本不可能的空间。我在电视节目《不可思议》（That’s Incredible）里见过类似的表演：一位身高六英尺、体重两百磅的瑜伽师把自己折叠进一个仅有玩具盒一半大小的有机玻璃立方体里。你可以透过透明的立方体墙看到他，沉静的面孔被像零件一样盘绕的四肢围在中央。艾莉森在我每次发现她陷入这种闪回状态时，也是如此扭曲。不同的是，这位瑜伽师却能脱身，而她做不到。比如她卡进马桶水箱下面时，我就得放掉水，拆掉水箱，把马桶连同底座拽出来才能把她放出来。

paragraphs.en: “We love our house,” I whispered through the side of the toybox. “It’s white with black shutters. You painted the front door red. You planted flowers in the beds.”
paragraphs.zh: “我们爱我们的房子，”我从玩具箱的一侧轻声说，“它是白色的，有黑色百叶窗。你把前门漆成了红色。你在花坛里种了花。”

paragraphs.en: “I’m thirty-two years old and you’re thirty-one. My hair’s falling out. We’re in the baby’s room on the second floor. We don’t know if it’s a boy or a girl yet. We don’t know if we want to know. The baby’s room is yellow, the same color it was when we bought the house. We don’t know what color to paint it yet.”
paragraphs.zh: “我三十二岁，你三十一岁。我的头发正在掉。我们在二楼的婴儿房。我们还不知道是男孩还是女孩。我们甚至不知道自己是否想知道。婴儿房是黄色的，是我们买房时的原色。我们还不知道该把它涂成什么颜色。”

paragraphs.en: Allison’s hand shot out from under the lid of the toybox and grabbed me by the collar.
paragraphs.zh: 艾莉森的手从玩具箱盖子下面猛地伸出来，抓住了我的衣领。

paragraphs.en: “Quiet,” she pleaded. “He’ll find us.”
paragraphs.zh: “安静，”她恳求道，“他会找到我们的。”

paragraphs.en: Allison didn’t know whether her father was dead or alive, let alone where he might be. I’d tried to find out, secretly. I’d given the spooks at the compound—the same men and women who, at the time, were spending all day and night searching the dark web for Osama bin Laden—Allison’s father’s name. I’d told them what Allison had told me—that her father had driven a van that smelled like buttermilk, that as a young man he’d aspired to join the San Francisco Ballet, and that, later in life, he’d impersonated a dentist in Milwaukee. No detail, no matter how incomplete or far-fetched, was irrelevant to their search. The spooks had plugged all this information into the algorithm—the same system that had delivered us Mohammed Atef, Khalid Sheikh Mohammed, and al-Zarqawi—and it had come up with a brother, working in a muffler shop in Palo Alto.
paragraphs.zh: 艾莉森不知道自己父亲是活着还是已经去世，更别提他可能在哪里了。我曾秘密去查找过。我把她父亲的名字告诉了复合体里的间谍——就是那时那些日以继夜在黑网里搜寻奥萨马·本·拉登的一男一女。她告诉我的那些细节我也一并说了：他年轻时开过一辆闻起来像牛奶渍味的面包车，年轻时曾想加入旧金山芭蕾舞团，后来又在密尔沃基假扮过牙科医生。无论多么零散或牵强的细节，都对他们的调查没有一条是不重要的。那些间谍把所有信息都输入了同一套算法——就是当年给我们带来穆罕默德·阿塔夫、哈立德·谢赫·穆罕默德和扎卡维的人——结果它给出了一个结果：一个在帕洛阿尔托一家消音器店工作的“兄弟”。

paragraphs.en: “He won’t find you,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “他找不到你的。”我说。

paragraphs.en: She pulled her hand back inside the toybox and its lid clapped shut.
paragraphs.zh: 她把手缩回去，玩具盒盖子咔嗒一声合上。

paragraphs.en: Rubber Hand with his hatchet, and Eye Patch with his M4, had done their work. The hatchet had fallen with the soft sound of dirt filling a hole. The M4 had discharged what looked like powerful gusts of wind. The pig men had crossed paths at the midpoint in the line of pigs, where each, in turn, deferred to the other as if he were opening a door for a lady, before continuing on to the other end. We weren’t supposed to watch.
paragraphs.zh: 橡皮手拿着他的短柄斧，眼罩拿着他的 M4，都已经做完了各自的事。短柄斧落下时发出一种像松软泥土填坑时的轻响。M4射出后，像是强风一样的气流撕过空气。那些‘猪人’在那列“猪”的中点相遇，彼此先后退让，好像是在为一位女士先开门一样，随后继续走向另一端。我们本不该看的。

paragraphs.en: “Y’all line up on a pig,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: “你们都排成一排上那头猪。”红胡子说。

paragraphs.en: A dozen pigs lay face up on the grass, hurt, doped, and smiling. I chose one with a freckled belly. A first-aid kit and a bottle of water lay on the grass beside him.
paragraphs.zh: 十多头猪仰躺在草地上，身上受了伤，神志不清，又还带着笑。我挑了一头肚皮上有雀斑的。草地边上放着一个急救包和一瓶水。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard held up a first-aid kit. “Same stuff we carry in the field, minus the fentanyl lollipop, which is a controlled substance.”
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子举起一个急救包。“我们在现场会带的那些东西都有，但没有吗啡类的‘棒棒糖’吗啡，这种东西是受管制物质。”

paragraphs.en: “Which is the tits,” Eye Patch said.
paragraphs.zh: “它在讲乳头，”眼罩说。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard continued. “When I say ‘Go,’ approach your wounded and do an assessment. Pat and swipe. Don’t try to put any outside stuff back inside. Leave that for the surgeons at the CASH. Your job is to prepare your brother for the journey. Project calm. Look him in the eye and tell him it’s going to be O.K. Tell him he’s going to make it. Raise your hand if you have questions.”
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子接着说：“当我说‘出发’的时候，你要靠近伤员并做评估。拍一拍、轻轻掸掉，不要试图把任何外来的东西再塞回去。那留给CASH的外科医生来做。你的任务是把兄弟准备好，帮助他继续路途。保持镇静。看着他的眼睛，对他说：一切都会好的。告诉他他能撑过去。你有问题就举手。”

paragraphs.en: No one raised his hand.
paragraphs.zh: 没有人举起手来。

paragraphs.en: My pig was heavier than I’d expected. His practically nonexistent neck bent in a very human way. Otherwise, I couldn’t find anything wrong with him. I raised my hand.
paragraphs.zh: 我那头猪比我预想的更重。他几乎没有脖子，脖子弯得像人类那样很自然。除此之外，我看不出他有什么不对劲。我举起了手。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard knelt next to me, and light shone from his knees. “Whatcha got?” he asked.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子在我身边跪下，光从他的膝盖上投射出来。“你拿到什么情况了？”他问。

paragraphs.en: “I don’t know,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “我不知道，”我说。

paragraphs.en: The pig looked at me out of one eye.
paragraphs.zh: 那头猪用一只眼睛看着我。

paragraphs.en: “Hey, buddy,” I said. “Where’s it hurt?”
paragraphs.zh: “嘿，伙计，”我说，“哪里疼？”

paragraphs.en: The pig blew a pink snot bubble out of one nostril. Red Beard pointed at it.
paragraphs.zh: 这头猪从一个鼻孔里喷出一个粉红色的鼻涕泡。红胡子指了指它。

paragraphs.en: “What’s that tell you?” he asked.
paragraphs.zh: “那说明了什么？”他问道。

paragraphs.en: “Collapsed lung?” I guessed.
paragraphs.zh: “是气胸吗？”我猜道。

paragraphs.en: “Right on,” Red Beard said. “Now what?”
paragraphs.zh: “猜对了，”红胡子说，“那现在怎么办？”

paragraphs.en: Inside the first-aid kit was a prepackaged needle that could be used to create a vent in the case of a collapsed lung. I peeled open this package, removed the needle, and held it between my teeth. I felt for the pig’s ribs with my fingertips. Into a soft spot between two ribs, I drove the needle home. There was a pop. The pig sighed and relaxed.
paragraphs.zh: 急救箱里有一支一次性包装的针，用于在怀疑气胸时开一个减压孔。我撕开了包装，取出针并夹在牙间。然后我用指尖去摸那只猪的肋骨。在两根肋骨之间的一处柔软地方，我把针扎了进去。针刺入的一刻“啪”地一声。那头猪轻轻叹了口气，然后放松下来。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard nudged me aside. He put his ear to the pig’s chest and listened for a heartbeat. He pried open the pig’s eyelids and stared into his giant pupils.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子把我轻轻推开。他把耳朵贴在那头猪的胸口上听心跳。然后他撬开猪的眼皮，盯着那双硕大的瞳孔。

paragraphs.en: “He’s gone,” he said.
paragraphs.zh: “他没了。”他说。

paragraphs.en: The pig men huddled by the tower. Eye Patch toed the grass and Rubber Hand hummed “Dixie” while Red Beard wrote in a little green notebook with a stubby red pencil.
paragraphs.zh: “红胡子”身旁的那些猪人（指涉“猪人”这一群体的人）正围在塔边。艾普·佩奇（Eye Patch）踮着脚在草地上蹲着，橡胶手（Rubber Hand）则哼着《Dixie》，而红胡子用一支短红色铅笔在一本小小的绿色笔记本上写字。

paragraphs.en: It was the same notebook-pencil combination that I used on missions downrange. I wrote call signs, frequencies, rendezvous coördinates, Zulu times, and Julian dates in that notebook’s blue-lined pages. Just strings of numbers, mostly, that would otherwise be shoved out of short-term memory by more numbers. But, when I looked back through those notebooks, the way the numbers slanted or grew large or turned shaky across the page told me all I needed to know about the night on which they’d been written, and therefore served as a kind of memory.
paragraphs.zh: 这正是我在前线任务中也在用的那本笔记本和铅笔组合。我在这本笔记本的蓝色横线页上写下了电台呼号、频率、会合坐标、祖鲁时（Zulu time）和儒略日（Julian date）。主要都是一串串数字，通常会被更多的数字瞬间挤出短期记忆。可每当我回头翻这些笔记本，那些数字在纸面上倾斜、变大、或抖动的方式，就已向我说明了它们当晚书写时的情况，于是它们便成了一种记忆。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard waved me over. He pointed the eraser of his pencil at my dead pig, lying in an oval of flat grass.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子朝我挥了挥手。他用铅笔上的橡皮头指向我的那头死猪，那头猪正躺在一片平坦草地形成的椭圆形区域里。

paragraphs.en: “Minus this one, your troop don’t make the cut,” he told me.
paragraphs.zh: “少了这只，你这支小队就达不到标准。”他对我说。

paragraphs.en: “You get that guy who saved two pigs?” Eye Patch asked.
paragraphs.zh: “你找到了那个人吗？他救了两头猪。”眼罩问道。

paragraphs.en: New Guy, in his exuberance, had patched a sucking chest wound on one pig then clamped another pig’s severed artery.
paragraphs.zh: 新手在兴奋中先给一头猪处理了一个吸气性胸部伤口，接着又夹住了另一头猪断裂的动脉。

paragraphs.en: “I got him,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: “我抓到了他，”红胡子说。

paragraphs.en: “We could round up?” Rubber Hand suggested.
paragraphs.zh: “我们把他围起来？”橡胶手建议道。

paragraphs.en: “This ain’t tiddlywinks,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: “这可不是玩拇指游戏啊。”红胡子说。

paragraphs.en: “Well, I guess that’s that,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “嗯，我想就是这样了，”我说。

paragraphs.en: “See? Isn’t this better than scalding-hot porridge back at our house?”
paragraphs.zh: “看到了吧？这难道不比我们家那碗滚烫烫的粥更好吗？”

paragraphs.en: Cartoon by Pia Guerra and Ian Boothby
paragraphs.zh: 插图作者：Pia Guerra 与 Ian Boothby

paragraphs.en: None of them acted like they’d heard me.
paragraphs.zh: 他们一个人都没有表现得像是听见了我。

paragraphs.en: “We could redo the first thing,” Rubber Hand said.
paragraphs.zh: Rubber Hand说：“我们可以把第一件事重做一遍。”

paragraphs.en: “Then we’ll run out of pigs for the last thing,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: “那我们到最后这件事的时候就会没猪了。”红胡子说。

paragraphs.en: “If I leave now, I can get to the farm and back before you’re done with the next thing,” Eye Patch said.
paragraphs.zh: “如果我现在离开，我能在你做完下一件事之前赶到农场再回来。”Eye Patch说。

paragraphs.en: “This can-do attitude?” I interrupted. “This make-it-happen-at-all-costs? It’s unnatural. We gotta stop.”
paragraphs.zh: “这股‘干就完了’的作风？‘不惜一切要做到’这心态？这不自然。我们必须停下来。”

paragraphs.en: “You take one-sixty-five down to sixty-eight?” Rubber Hand asked Eye Patch.
paragraphs.zh: “你要把一百六十五降到六十八？”Rubber Hand问道。

paragraphs.en: “This time of day,” Eye Patch said.
paragraphs.zh: “这时候，”眼斑说道。

paragraphs.en: “Leave the trailer here, and put, like, six pigs in the cab,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: “把拖车留在这儿，然后像平常一样，把六头猪放进驾驶室。”红胡子说。

paragraphs.en: “I’d knock ’em out first,” Rubber Hand said.
paragraphs.zh: “我先把它们先打晕。”橡皮手说道。

paragraphs.en: Just then my pig jumped to its feet and shook the wet blades of grass off its back.
paragraphs.zh: 就在这时，我的那头猪跳了起来，抖掉背上湿漉漉的草。

paragraphs.en: “There you go,” Red Beard said.
paragraphs.zh: “给你，”红胡子说。

paragraphs.en: The sun was on its way up, and birds were chirping in the bare branches of the oak tree outside the window. Allison was still in the toybox, curled up, I imagined, with her knees at her chin and her elbows at her ankles. I talked to her the way the therapist had recommended—slowly and steadily, with a focus on our immediate surroundings and the current situation, in hopes that this intonation of our state of affairs would end her flashback and return her to the present day. I had just started talking about the future.
paragraphs.zh: 太阳刚刚升起，窗外那棵橡树光秃秃的树枝上，鸟儿正在啁啾鸣叫。艾莉森还在玩具盒里蜷着身子，我想象她是把膝盖抱到下巴，把肘部弯到脚踝。我按治疗师建议的方式跟她说话——慢慢地、稳定地，把注意力放在我们周围的环境和此刻的处境上，希望这种平稳的语调能让她的创伤性回忆中断，让她回到现实。我刚刚开始谈起未来。

paragraphs.en: “I want our lives to calm down,” I said. “I want us to sit on the porch, watch the sun set over the canal, let our kids play in the yard, and...”
paragraphs.zh: “我想让我们的生活平静下来，”我说，“我想让我们坐在门廊上，看着太阳落在运河上，让孩子们在院子里玩耍，然后……”

paragraphs.en: Boom! Allison’s foot broke through one side of the toybox. Her bare and scraped-up leg hung out from mid-thigh.
paragraphs.zh: 砰！艾莉森的一只脚踩出了玩具盒的一侧。她赤裸、带着擦伤的腿从大腿中部露了出来。

paragraphs.en: She elbowed out the front panel of the toybox, then rolled out of the wreckage and onto her hands and knees. Her hair was matted and her eyes were wild.
paragraphs.zh: 她用手肘把玩具箱的前面板掀开，然后从残骸里滚出来，跪在了地上。她的头发乱糟糟的，眼神很狂乱。

paragraphs.en: “There are snakes in the back yard,” she said.
paragraphs.zh: “后院里有蛇。”她说。

paragraphs.en: “What kind of snakes?” I asked. I’d never seen one.
paragraphs.zh: “什么样的蛇？”我问道。我从没见过。

paragraphs.en: “Cottonmouths. They come up from the canal and sun themselves on the grass. You need to get rid of them.”
paragraphs.zh: “棉口蛇。它们从运河那边游过来，爬到草地上晒太阳。你得把它们清除掉。”

paragraphs.en: “As soon as I come home from work,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “我一下班就回家，”我说。

paragraphs.en: “No. Do it now,” she said.
paragraphs.zh: “不要。现在就做。”她说。

paragraphs.en: The final scenario was Burning Down the House. Eye Patch carried the last pig up the stairs of the tower all the way to the roof, where he laid it down on the gritty, sunbaked surface. Then he looked over the edge and down at Red Beard, who was looking up at him from a gas valve the size of a ship’s wheel.
paragraphs.zh: 最后一个情景是《烧毁房屋》。眼罩把最后一头猪一路抬上了塔楼的楼梯，直到屋顶，放在又粗糙又晒得发烫的地面上。然后他俯下身看向下方的红胡子，红胡子正从一个像船舵那么大的天然气阀门前抬头看着他。

paragraphs.en: “That does it!” Eye Patch said.
paragraphs.zh: “算了吧！”眼罩说。

paragraphs.en: Red Beard made a mark in his notebook.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子在他的小本子上做了个记号。

paragraphs.en: “Come on down!” he said.
paragraphs.zh: “快下来！”他说。

paragraphs.en: Before we’d commandeered it for our purposes, this tower had been a training aid for the fire department. As such, it had been a placeholder for all homes in the area that might one day catch fire. So it was also a proxy for the two-story Colonial, on the banks of the muddy canal, where Allison and I lived. And it stood in for the house three doors down, identical to ours in every way save its crooked shutters and dead shrubs, where the dishevelled little girl from the bus stop lived.
paragraphs.zh: 在我们把它拿来用以前，这座塔一直是消防局的训练辅助设施。因此，它一直作为这一带可能有一天会失火的房屋的替代体。它还替代了我们所在的那座位于浑浊运河岸边的两层殖民式住宅。而且，它也代表了向前走三户的那间屋子——这间屋子在各方面都和我们家一模一样，只是它的百叶窗歪斜、灌木枯萎——那间屋子里住着一位巴士站口常见的、头发乱蓬蓬的小女孩。

paragraphs.en: Ted Waters sat on the grass near the fence. He’d undone the rubber bands that held his mechanical leg in place and set it aside. He was scratching the dirt where his shin would have been, watching the situation develop. As Eye Patch descended the stairs of the tower, whistling a carefree, shuffling tune, Ted stood up and hopped over to me.
paragraphs.zh: 泰德·沃特斯坐在篱笆旁的草地上。他解开了固定他那只机械腿的橡皮筋，并把腿放在一边。那条腿本该在小腿位置的地方，他正用手指抓着泥土，同时观察着局势的发展。当眼罩男（Eye Patch）一边哼着轻松自在、拖着步伐的哨子曲子，一边从塔楼的楼梯往下走时，泰德站起身，向我一小步一小步地走了过来。

paragraphs.en: “Do something!” he said.
paragraphs.zh: “去做点什么！”他说。

paragraphs.en: “Like what?” I asked.
paragraphs.zh: “比如做什么？”我问。

paragraphs.en: “You got a big yard. Take these pigs in. Let ’em grow old.”
paragraphs.zh: “你那边院子很大。把这些小猪赶进来。让它们慢慢长老吧。”

paragraphs.en: Earlier that morning, while I was standing in the back yard with a shovel, cottonmouths had come up from the canal. They’d slithered uphill through the grass toward the house. They’d lunged at me with their fangs out. I’d lowered the shovel and, one by one, chopped off eight heads. I’d left the heads in the grass as a warning to the rest.
paragraphs.zh: 那天早些时候，我站在后院里拿着铁锹时，棉口蛇从运河那边爬了上来。它们沿着草地往屋子方向向上游。它们张着毒牙向我猛扑过来。我把铁锹放低，一次又一次地砍掉了八个蛇头。然后我把那些头留在草丛里，作为对其他蛇的警告。

paragraphs.en: “My wife doesn’t want any critters in the back yard,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “我妻子不想后院里有任何小动物，”我说道。

paragraphs.en: “Stand clear!” Red Beard hollered.
paragraphs.zh: “快躲开！”红胡子大喊。

paragraphs.en: New Guy stood facing the tower with his sleeves rolled up, hands on his hips, and chest out. He shouted at us over his shoulder, “Who’s with me?”
paragraphs.zh: 新手站在塔前，卷着袖子，双手叉腰，胸口微挺。他越过肩膀朝我们喊道：“谁跟我一起？”

paragraphs.en: Red Beard turned the valve on a six-inch gas line. This fat line fed a series of narrower pipes in the tower, which ran along the corners of every floor, wall, and ceiling, and were perforated to spray gas into the rooms. With the valve wide open this system made a noise like a hovering spaceship.
paragraphs.zh: 红胡子打开了一根六英寸燃气管道上的阀门。 这根粗管给塔里一系列更细的管道供气，这些细管沿着每层、每面墙和天花板的角落延伸，并在上面开孔把燃气喷入各个房间。阀门全开时，这个系统会发出像悬停的飞船在嗡鸣般的噪音。

paragraphs.en: Bing Thomas ran over to me and nudged Ted Waters aside. The expression on Bing’s face alternated between satanic and serene. His crooked nose honked like a kazoo.
paragraphs.zh: 宾格·托马斯跑过来，把泰德·沃特斯推到一边。宾格脸上的表情在像撒旦一样和祥和之间来回变换。他那歪歪扭扭的鼻子像卡祖笛一样发出吱呀声。

paragraphs.en: “I ain’t running into no burning building,” he said.
paragraphs.zh: “我可不想往着着火的楼里冲。”他说。

paragraphs.en: “Fear is an illusion!” New Guy announced.
paragraphs.zh: “恐惧是一种幻觉！”新来的人宣布道。

paragraphs.en: The igniter was wired to a red button inside a little black box mounted to a wooden post. Red Beard opened the door to this box and pushed the button. The fuse clicked softly. The spark sounded like a stick breaking in half. Then—whoomp!—flames jumped from the tower’s open windows and stood straight up against the light-blue sky.
paragraphs.zh: 点火器接在一块小黑盒子里的红色按钮上，而那只盒子固定在一根木桩上。红胡子打开了盒门并按下按钮。导火索轻轻“啪”地一声响。火花像木棒折断时的声音。然后——“whoomp!”——火焰从塔楼敞开的窗户里蹿了出来，向着淡蓝色的天空笔直竖起。

paragraphs.en: Heat from the fire warmed my face. It toasted my chest through my shirt. It sucked a draft out of the swamp which smelled like rotten eggs. Pig screams were no different from our screams.
paragraphs.zh: 火光烘热了我的脸，也把我的胸口烤得发烫，热量穿过衬衫直抵皮肤。它把一股恶臭的沼泽冷风吸走了，那股味道像腐烂的鸡蛋。猪的惨叫和我们的惨叫并没有两样。

paragraphs.en: Bing Thomas ran into the tower with one eye shut. Ted Waters reattached his leg on the fly. New Guy didn’t budge, so the boys and I parted around him and came together on the other side. ♦
paragraphs.zh: 宾·托马斯一只眼睛闭着冲进塔里。特德·沃特斯就在途中替他接好了腿。新来的那家伙纹风不动，于是男孩们和我绕过他，在另一边汇合。♦
